title: Part VI-A - COLLAPSE: a quantitative method for detecting systemic bias
author: Atkinson, James D.
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
Project website: https://www.integrodynamics.org/ 
 This paper introduces a quantitative method for detecting systemic bias and institutional distortion through the analysis of statistically implausible multi-domain outcome patterns. It formalises  collapse analysis  as a reproducible evidential test for determining when benign organisational explanations become probabilistically untenable. 
 The framework proceeds in layered stages: an initial independence assessment over observed domain outcomes; conservative dependency stress-testing via exchangeable and bounded-correlation models; and a Bayesian update formalising posterior convergence under directional collapse. Where the probability of the observed configuration under the admissible benign model class falls below actuarial materiality, the framework treats the event as a quantitative signal of systemic distortion. 
 Collapse analysis is not presented as proof of motive or intent. It is the formal rejection of the hypothesis that a coordinated adverse configuration could reasonably arise by chance under any plausible integrity-preserving governance regime. The method operates entirely on probabilistic structure under explicit assumptions and does not depend on testimonial evidence, narrative reconstruction, or actor-level attribution. 
 The framework is implemented as a five-stage operational protocol and demonstrated on a fully synthetic multi-domain case study. The resulting collapse event provides a replicable evidential foundation for inference in legal, regulatory, and governance contexts, enabling principled escalation from anomaly detection to systemic diagnosis. 
 This paper forms the formal detection layer of a wider collapse framework developed in Parts VI-B, VI-C, and VI-D, which extend the analysis to institutional regime structure, interior system deformation, and civilisational inadmissibility. 
  Keywords:  collapse analysis; systemic bias; forensic statistics; multi-domain outcomes; evidential probability; Bayesian inference; dependency modelling; Monte Carlo methods; decision thresholds; governance systems; institutional failure; integrity regimes; regime transition; admissibility; harm conservation; structural collapse.
